gabby_xz_xiang.name: Gabby XZ Xiang
gabby_xz_xiang.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Gabby XZ Xiang is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 3 titles across 18 tournaments and 48 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Gabby keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Gabby is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Gabby has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Erela Liu (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (13 total events in GD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Pranav Vivek (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Gabby has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Gabby isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 3 titles, Gabby has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
gabby_xz_xiang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Gabby XZ Xiang, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Gabby has 48 career entries across 18 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Gabby saga began at 2023 South CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 Peak Sports South OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Gabby's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Gabby actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 7 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Gabby plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Gabby can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Gabby has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Gabby's texts. The go-to partner is Erela Liu (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Yuancheng Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Gabby's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Gabby has shown up 6 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Gabby has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Gabby has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Gabby showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Gabby traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Gabby entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Gabby paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 48 entries, and 45 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Gabby looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Gabby's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Gabby has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Gabby. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

What makes Gabriel especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

In doubles, Gabriel has formed effective partnerships:
  • Katie Moy (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
gabriel_sutanto.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Gabriel Sutanto, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Gabriel is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 Midwest CRC, and so far, the only thing Gabriel has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Gabriel saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Gabriel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Gabriel has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Gabriel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Gabriel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Gabriel entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Gabriel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Gabriel is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Gabriel wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Gabriel, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Ganesh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ganesh, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ganesh. We will be here, taking notes.

garret_tan.name: Garret Tan
garret_tan.summary:
Hailing from California, Garret Tan is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (XD U13, BS U19) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 9 career titles across 16 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Garret is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Garret competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (XD U13, BS U19, 2019, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U19 BD, U17 XD, U19 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BD U15) • 1 ORC title.

  • Jaden Ke Hong Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (2 events, best: #3)

Garret is peaking at the right time in 2024-2025, with 4 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Garret is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Garret has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Garret has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 6 seasons and 9 titles, Garret has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
garret_tan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Garret Tan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Garret is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (XD U13, BS U19), which means Garret has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Garret's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 40 career entries to discuss and a 22% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Garret saga began at 2019 Pan Am Junior, where Garret burst onto the scene with a title in XD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Garret has delivered on some of those promises: 9 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Garret promised and what Garret has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. This 2024-2025 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 9 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Garret experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Garret has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Garret entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BD U15, BS U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Garret does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Garret has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 9 wins means Garret is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Garret plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Garret can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Garret has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Garret's texts. The go-to partner is Tian Qi Alan Zhang (6 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19 — we hope Tian Qi Alan Zhang has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Garret of is playing it safe. Garret has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Garret has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Garret has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Garret is not just losing locally — Garret is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 22% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tan family travels cross-country so Garret can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Garret has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Garret showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19, which means Garret traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 40 entries, and 31 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Garret looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Garret's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Garret has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Garret. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

geoffray_lee.name: Geoffray Lee
geoffray_lee.summary:
Out of Missouri, Geoffray Lee is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 16 tournaments and 46 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Geoffray keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Geoffray has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Geoffray especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Geoffray adapts to anyone:
  • Darren Teu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U17 (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Serena Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (5 events, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Arya Prakash Mirani (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Meggie Chang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Geoffray has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Geoffray has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Geoffray brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
geoffray_lee.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Geoffray Lee, from Missouri, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 46 career entries in 16 tournaments, Geoffray has 2 titles — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 22 entries where Geoffray drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 46 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Geoffray saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, where Geoffray burst onto the scene with a title in BD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Geoffray has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Geoffray promised and what Geoffray has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Geoffray actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Geoffray is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Geoffray experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Geoffray has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Geoffray does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Geoffray has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Geoffray is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Geoffray plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Geoffray is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Geoffray and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Geoffray needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Geoffray has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Geoffray's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Serena Yang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Serena Yang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Arnav Tiwari has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Geoffray's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Geoffray entered 6 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Geoffray's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Geoffray and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Geoffray has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Geoffray has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Geoffray is not just losing locally — Geoffray is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lee family travels cross-country so Geoffray can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Geoffray has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Geoffray showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Geoffray traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Geoffray entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Geoffray paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 46 entries, and 44 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Geoffray looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Geoffray's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Geoffray has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Geoffray. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

grace_cheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Grace Cheng, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Grace is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (GS U13), which means Grace has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Grace's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Grace went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Grace represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 58 career entries and a 14% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 58 entries worth of material.

The Grace saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2024 Frisco South ORC in GD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Grace's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 15 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Grace experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Grace has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Grace entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Grace does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Grace has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 8 wins means Grace is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Grace plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Grace is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Grace and suddenly the wins start appearing — 8 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Grace needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Grace has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Grace's texts. The most successful partnership is with Zelenia Shen — 11 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Zelenia Shen deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Zelenia Shen a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Leo Ye has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Grace entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Grace's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Grace and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 9 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Grace has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Tournament committees have handed Grace a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Grace has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in GS U13, where Grace was seeded #1 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Grace is not just losing locally — Grace is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cheng family travels cross-country so Grace can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Grace has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Grace showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13, which means Grace traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Grace entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Grace paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 58 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Grace looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Grace's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Grace has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Grace. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Grace Yueyi Cheng is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U11 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Grace competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
grace_yueyi_cheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Grace Yueyi Cheng, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Grace is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 South ORC, and so far, the only thing Grace has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Grace saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Grace plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Grace has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Grace showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U11, which means Grace traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Grace is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Grace wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Grace, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

grace_zhao.name: Grace Zhao
grace_zhao.summary:
Out of California, Grace Zhao keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 3 times, currently competing at the U13 level, building a strong foundation across 10 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

What makes Grace especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Grace has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Ethan Xiao-ze Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events)
  • Elaine Yan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5)

Grace has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Grace has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Grace brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
grace_zhao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Grace Zhao, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Grace has 29 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Grace saga began at 2022 NW ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Grace plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Grace has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Grace's texts. The go-to partner is Harrison Zhang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Harrison Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Grace has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Grace is not just losing locally — Grace is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhao family travels cross-country so Grace can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Grace has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Grace showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Grace traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Grace entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 49th. That is an average placement of 31st, which is a polite way of saying Grace paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Grace keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Grace has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Grace, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Grace. We will be here, taking notes.

grace_zichen_qu_zhao.name: Grace Zichen Qu Zhao

Grace is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Grace adapts to anyone:
  • Ethan Xiao-ze Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Trisha Mehta (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Rocky Khuu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5)

Grace has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Grace's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.

That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Grace paid full price to get absolutely demolished.
